text: The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric artery, securing it with hemo-lock clips at its origin. After dividing the artery, a medial to lateral dissection plane is developed anterior to Girota's fascia, with traction and counter traction visibly aiding the procedure.

The surgeon performs dissection laterally and toward the pancreas at the origin of the enteric vein. The upper border of the pancreas is exposed, and the peritoneal layer covering the pancreas is carefully divided. The inferior mesenteric vein is divided between clips, avoiding undue traction with the robotic arm to prevent bleeding. A swab is placed over the pancreas to serve as an anatomical landmark, completing step one of splenic flexure mobilization.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection until reaching the splenocolic attachments, using robotic arms R-II and R-III for traction and counter-traction. An assistant holds the colon to improve exposure. Monopolar diathermy is used to join the medial and lateral layers, and the omental attachments are carefully divided. The swab placed at the lateral edge of the pancreas is visible, marking the completion of the second step of splenic flexure mobilization.

The surgeon exposes the lesser sac and separates the omentum from the transverse colon. Robotic arm R-II is declutched, and the instrument is removed to minimize conflict. Traction is provided by the assistant on the colon, while robotic arm R-III holds the omentum up and toward the spleen. The posterior wall of the stomach is inspected to confirm entry into the lesser sac. Dissection is carried laterally to join previously created layers on the right of the flexure. A swab over the surface of the pancreas is visible, and the peritoneal layer above it is divided.

The surgeon continues the mobilization of the left colon medially by pulling the colon downward while holding the omentum upward, facilitating further exposure. The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg with a right-sided tilt, which presents challenges for complete mobilization of the splenic flexure.
